proposals.title: Spatially Differentiated Gray Water Footprints: Integrating Local Water Quality Standards and Dilution Capacity for Livestock and Crop Production
proposals.motivation: The standard gray water footprint (GWF) methodology, as defined in the Water Footprint Assessment Manual (2011), assumes uniform ambient water quality standards and unlimited dilution capacity, which misrepresents real-world water pollution impacts. This oversimplification is particularly problematic for agricultural water use assessments, as seen in Hoekstra's prior work on livestock and crop systems in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin, where local environmental conditions vary drastically. A spatially explicit GWF is critical for accurate resource productivity comparisons and for informing sustainable water allocation and pollution mitigation policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that calculating gray water footprints using spatially variable, locally relevant water quality standards and river-specific dilution capacities will yield significantly different, and more policy-relevant, estimates of water productivity and virtual water flows for key agricultural commodities compared to the standard uniform method.
proposals.method: The research will extend the existing GWF framework by developing a high-resolution geospatial model. For selected case studies (e.g., Kenyan livestock systems, Yellow River Basin crop production), we will compile spatially explicit data on: (1) pollutant loads from manure and fertilizers using life-cycle inventory data; (2) local ambient water quality standards (e.g., national maximum contaminant concentrations for nitrogen, phosphorus); and (3) natural dilution capacity derived from river discharge data (e.g., from GRDC databases) and catchment characteristics. The model will calculate GWF at the sub-catchment level using the formula GWF = L / (c_max - c_nat), where L is load, c_max is the local standard, and c_nat is the local background concentration, all defined per spatial unit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a major methodological advancement in water footprint accounting by introducing a spatially differentiated GWF. It will deliver more accurate and location-specific assessments of water pollution impacts from agriculture, enabling better comparisons of water and land productivity between regions and production systems. The results will directly inform scenarios for future resource use that account for local assimilation limits, supporting more targeted and effective water quality management.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies, including Hoekstra's own work, have applied the standard GWF, none have systematically integrated spatially variable legal water quality standards and natural dilution capacities into the core calculation on a catchment scale for agricultural commodities. This moves beyond a simple volume-based assessment to a value-based impact assessment of water pollution.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model of Farmer and Consumer Decisions to Simulate Water Footprint Dynamics in Kenyan Mixed Crop-Livestock Systems
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and virtual water assessments often rely on static, top-down scenarios, missing the critical feedback between local resource productivity, farmer adaptation, and market signals. Understanding how individual decisions aggregate to shape regional water sustainability is vital for effective policy, especially in water-scarce regions like Kenya where crop and livestock production compete for limited land and water.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simulating heterogeneous agent behaviors—including farmers responding to water productivity and consumers shifting diets—will reveal non-linear thresholds and trade-offs in regional water footprints that are invisible to aggregate scenario analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a structure similar to established frameworks like NetLogo or Repast. Farmer agents will make land-use and livestock management decisions based on rules derived from Hoekstra's pre-2026 Kenyan productivity data, optimizing for profit under water constraints. Consumer agents will influence markets through demand elasticity for water-intensive goods. The model will be validated against historical water footprint data from the Yellow River Basin and Kenyan studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a dynamic, bottom-up simulation tool to explore future resource use, linking micro-level decisions to macro-level water sustainability indicators. It will advance the field by quantifying how behavioral heterogeneity and local adaptation strategies influence the volume and value of virtual water flows in a coupled human-natural system.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs are used in land-use science, their application to integrate water footprint accounting with empirically grounded decision rules for both production (crop/livestock) and consumption agents represents a novel synthesis of Hoekstra's established footprint methods with complex systems simulation.

proposals.title: Dynamic Environmental Flow Requirements for Sustainable Water Allocation: A Spatially-Explicit, Ecosystem-Demand Model for River Basins
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and allocation assessments, including those for livestock and crop production, often rely on static environmental flow requirement (EFR) thresholds (e.g., the widely used 80% rule). These fixed percentages fail to capture the seasonal variability of river flows and the specific ecological needs of different river reaches, potentially leading to unsustainable water abstractions or inefficient water productivity analyses. Integrating dynamic EFRs is crucial for accurately assessing the water sustainability of agricultural production systems and future resource use scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing fixed EFR thresholds with dynamic, ecosystem-demand models that vary by season and ecological context will yield significantly different and more ecologically sustainable estimates of blue water availability for human activities, including livestock and crop production, within river basins.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially-explicit hydrological-ecological model building on pre-2026 methods like the WaterGAP model and the Ecological Limits of Hydrologic Alteration (ELOHA) framework. The model will integrate high-resolution seasonal hydrological data with ecological response curves for key indicator species or ecosystem functions, defined per river reach. Water availability for human use will be calculated as natural runoff minus these dynamic EFRs. We will apply this model to the Yellow River Basin (linking to Hoekstra's prior work) and a major Kenyan river basin (linking to livestock productivity studies), comparing water availability and sustainability indicators against results from static EFR methods under various future climate and land-use scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological framework for calculating dynamic environmental flows, directly enhancing the accuracy of water footprint and virtual water flow assessments for agricultural commodities. It will offer basin-specific insights for water allocation planning that better balance ecosystem health and agricultural water productivity, contributing to more robust scenarios for future resource use.
proposals.novelty: While the concept of variable EFRs exists, this proposal innovates by directly integrating a dynamic, ecosystem-demand-driven EFR model into the water footprint and agricultural productivity assessment framework pioneered by Hoekstra, moving beyond simple hydrological rules-of-thumb to a process-based ecological model.

proposals.title: Dynamic population mapping for water scarcity assessment: Integrating seasonal migration and urbanization in the Kenyan context
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and scarcity assessments, including our own work in Kenya, often rely on static, administrative-level population data. This fails to capture critical intra-annual dynamics, such as pastoralist seasonal migration and rapid urbanization, which redistribute water demand and exposure to scarcity across basins. A static view risks misallocating virtual water flows and misinforming policies on future resource use under climate and demographic change.
proposals.hypothesis: Accounting for seasonal and long-term population mobility will significantly alter the spatial and temporal mapping of people to water-scarce basins in Kenya, leading to a more accurate assessment of pressure on water resources for both crop and livestock production systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatio-temporal population redistribution model for Kenya, combining high-resolution census data, satellite-derived nighttime lights for urban growth, and movement data from mobile network operators (where available pre-2026) or literature-based migration calendars for pastoralist communities. This dynamic population layer will be integrated into a revised Water Footprint Assessment framework to calculate monthly basin-specific water scarcity indices (WSI) for the period 2010-2025. Scenarios will compare static vs. dynamic population assumptions on water scarcity for people and for key agricultural sectors (e.g., dairy, maize).
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodology for integrating human mobility into hydrological sustainability assessments. It will yield more realistic estimates of how many people and which production systems are exposed to water scarcity at different times of the year, directly informing Kenya's national water and agricultural development plans.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have noted the importance of dynamics, this work explicitly models and quantifies the impact of both seasonal migration and secular urbanization trends on water footprint and scarcity metrics, moving beyond a correction factor to a integrated spatio-temporal assessment framework.

proposals.title: Unmasking Scarcity: A Sub-Basin Analysis of Water and Land Productivity Hotspots in Heterogeneous River Basins
proposals.motivation: Water footprint and resource productivity assessments are often aggregated at the river-basin scale, a practice recommended for water management. However, in large, heterogeneous basins, this averaging may obscure critical local scarcities and unsustainable resource use, leading to policies that are ineffective or even exacerbate local crises. This is particularly relevant for agricultural and livestock systems, where water and land productivity vary dramatically with local agro-ecology and management.
proposals.hypothesis: River-basin scale aggregation of water and land use metrics significantly masks sub-basin hotspots of water scarcity and low resource productivity, especially for water-intensive crop and livestock production systems. Identifying these hotspots is essential for targeted, sustainable resource management.
proposals.method: The study will apply the Water Footprint Assessment framework at multiple spatial scales within one or two large, heterogeneous river basins (e.g., the Yellow River Basin, Kenya's Tana River Basin). Using pre-2026 data (e.g., FAO, hydrological models, agricultural statistics), we will first calculate basin-average blue and green water footprints and land/productivity ratios for key crops and livestock, following Hoekstra's established methods. We will then systematically disaggregate these calculations to sub-basin or administrative unit levels. Hotspots will be identified by comparing local water scarcity indices and resource productivities against basin averages and sustainability thresholds, using spatial statistical analysis to quantify the degree of masking.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a methodological demonstration of the risks of spatial aggregation in water footprint studies. It will yield concrete, spatially explicit evidence of hidden resource pressures, offering a refined lens for policy targeting. The findings will advance the field of sustainable resource use by strengthening the spatial resolution and policy relevance of footprint indicators.
proposals.novelty: While water footprint studies exist at various scales, this work explicitly tests the *information loss* caused by basin-level aggregation—a common practice—and quantifies the mismatch between aggregate basin sustainability and local realities for integrated crop-livestock systems.

proposals.title: Optimizing Basin-Scale Water Productivity: A Network Flow Model for Allocating Scarce Water Among Competing Agricultural Sectors
proposals.motivation: Growing water scarcity and competing demands from crops and livestock challenge sustainable resource use in river basins. Pre-2026 water footprint and land productivity studies, like those in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin, quantify pressures but lack prescriptive frameworks for optimal allocation. Applying formal optimization from logistics can bridge this gap, moving from descriptive accounting to actionable management.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a minimum-cost network flow optimization model to a river basin system will identify water allocation pathways that maximize aggregate economic water productivity (value per unit water) across interconnected crop and livestock production zones, outperforming current or proportional allocation rules.
proposals.method: We will model the river basin as a directed graph with nodes representing water sources, demand zones (for specific crops and livestock), and sinks. Arcs represent flow possibilities with capacities (e.g., transfer limits) and costs. Building on Hoekstra's water productivity data, the objective function will minimize the total 'cost' of water use, defined as the foregone economic value (inverse of water productivity). We will implement and solve the model using the Simplex algorithm or a dedicated network solver (e.g., the Network Simplex) within an operations research framework, applied to a case study basin like the Yellow River.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, optimization-based decision-support tool for basin planners to allocate water where it generates the most economic value. It will advance water footprint analysis from a diagnostic to a prescriptive science, offering concrete scenarios for improving system-wide water productivity while considering physical network constraints.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is established in hydrology for physical flow, its application to optimize economic water productivity—synthesizing water footprint data, crop/livestock value, and logistical network optimization—for agricultural water allocation between sectors is a novel interdisciplinary synthesis.

proposals.title: Diversifying water supply portfolios: A financial risk theory approach to mitigating basin-level scarcity risk
proposals.motivation: Water scarcity risk in river basins is increasing due to climate variability and demand growth, threatening agricultural and economic productivity. Current water footprint and virtual water flow analyses often assess volumetric use but do not formally quantify the risk exposure of a basin's water supply system to climatic and hydrological shocks. A robust, quantitative framework for managing this risk is urgently needed for sustainable resource planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a river basin's exposure to water scarcity risk can be significantly reduced by diversifying its water supply sources, analogous to how financial portfolio diversification reduces investment risk, and that this risk can be quantified using metrics from Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT).
proposals.method: We will adapt the Mean-Variance Portfolio (MVP) optimization framework from financial theory. Water sources (e.g., surface runoff, groundwater, reservoir storage, virtual water imports via crops/livestock) are treated as assets. Their 'returns' are annual available water volumes, characterized by historical time series (mean yield and variance/covariance). We will construct an 'efficient frontier' of water portfolios that minimize scarcity risk (variance) for a given level of expected water supply, using quadratic programming. The analysis will be applied to the Yellow River Basin, integrating crop and livestock water productivity data from Hoekstra's prior work to assess the risk-mitigation value of different production and trade scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal application of financial portfolio risk theory to physical water resource management at the basin scale. It will deliver a quantitative metric for basin water security and a decision-support tool for optimizing water allocation and virtual water trade strategies under uncertainty. It directly extends the water footprint concept into the domain of risk management.
proposals.novelty: While water footprint and virtual water studies are established, applying Modern Portfolio Theory to model water sources as a diversified investment portfolio to minimize scarcity risk is a novel methodological crossover. It moves beyond descriptive footprint accounting to prescriptive, risk-averse resource optimization.

proposals.title: Mapping intra-urban water scarcity: A monthly water footprint and scarcity assessment for Nairobi, Kenya
proposals.motivation: Global water scarcity assessments, including the water footprint scarcity index, are typically conducted at national or river basin scales, masking critical sub-national heterogeneity. Rapid urbanization, especially in water-stressed regions like Kenya, concentrates demand and creates localized hotspots of water stress and inequitable access within cities. A city-scale, temporally explicit assessment is urgently needed to inform targeted urban water management and infrastructure planning.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a monthly water footprint and scarcity framework at high spatial resolution within a major city will reveal significant intra-urban disparities in water stress, driven by the spatial distribution of water-intensive economic activities (e.g., livestock-based industries, crop processing) and residential consumption patterns, which are not captured by aggregate city-level analyses.
proposals.method: The method adapts Hoekstra's monthly blue water footprint scarcity framework to the city scale. We will delineate Nairobi's water supply catchments and sub-catchments using GIS. Monthly blue water footprints for key sectors (municipal supply, livestock processing, commercial agriculture) will be spatially disaggregated using high-resolution land use, industrial, and population data. These will be compared to locally available monthly blue water flows, derived from hydrological models and gauge data, to calculate a monthly water scarcity index per sub-catchment or district. Scenarios of urban growth and climate variability will be tested.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, sector-specific monthly water scarcity map for a major African city, directly linking local water consumption to available resources. It will advance the water footprint methodology by operationalizing it for intra-urban analysis. The findings will provide a quantitative evidence base for identifying priority areas for water conservation and infrastructure investment to enhance urban water security and equity.
proposals.novelty: While the water footprint scarcity index is established for larger regions, its application to dissect water stress *within* a single city, integrating spatially explicit data on both production (e.g., from livestock and crop value chains) and municipal supply, represents a significant downscaling and novel integration of the framework with urban geography.

proposals.title: Mapping Global Water Abundance and Surplus: Identifying Potential Source Basins for Sustainable Water Transfers
proposals.motivation: Water footprint and scarcity assessments predominantly focus on regions of deficit, framing water as a constraint. This deficit-centric view overlooks the strategic potential of regions with persistent water surplus. Identifying and quantifying these surpluses is crucial for informing long-term, large-scale water security strategies, including the feasibility of virtual water trade reorientation or physical water transfer projects, within planetary boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, high-resolution global analysis will reveal specific river basins with significant, reliable, and sustainable water surpluses, defined by consistent positive water yield after accounting for environmental flow requirements and local renewable resource use.
proposals.method: We will apply and extend the Water Footprint Assessment framework, inverting the typical scarcity analysis. Using pre-2026 global datasets (e.g., WaterGAP, FAO AQUASTAT, and climate reanalysis), we will calculate long-term (1990-2020) monthly blue water availability minus environmental flow requirements and current anthropogenic withdrawals. Surplus will be defined as the reliable, non-damaging excess. The analysis will be conducted at the river basin scale, employing statistical methods to distinguish persistent surplus from inter-annual variability, and will integrate land productivity metrics (inspired by Hoekstra's Kenya work) to assess the opportunity cost of water in surplus regions.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first global, basin-level inventory of sustainable water surplus, shifting the discourse from scarcity management to abundance sourcing. It will provide a quantitative, spatially-explicit evidence base for discussing water transfer potentials and inform scenarios on redirecting virtual water flows from high-surplus, low-productivity regions to high-deficit, high-productivity ones.
proposals.novelty: While water scarcity is well-mapped, a systematic global inventory of *sustainable surplus* for strategic resource planning is novel. It inverts the standard water footprint problem and integrates surplus quantification with considerations of local land-water productivity, moving beyond purely hydrological metrics.

proposals.title: Mapping and Diagnosing Chronic Water Surplus Basins: A Framework for Flood Risk Mitigation and Strategic Storage
proposals.motivation: Water footprint and scarcity assessments predominantly focus on deficits, often overlooking basins with persistent water surplus, which represent significant flood risks and lost opportunities for strategic water storage. In the pre-2026 context of increasing hydrological variability and extreme rainfall events, a systematic framework to identify and characterize these surplus basins is critical for integrated water resources management, complementing scarcity-focused analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inverting conventional water scarcity indicators can robustly identify river basins with chronic water surplus, and that cross-analyzing these surpluses with land use (e.g., cropland, pasture) and storage infrastructure data will reveal distinct typologies for flood risk and potential storage development.
proposals.method: We will apply the water footprint assessment framework, using long-term (e.g., 1990-2020) hydrological data to calculate the blue water footprint of production and available runoff at the basin scale. A 'surplus index' will be defined as the ratio of available blue water to the consumptive water footprint, inverted from standard scarcity indicators. Basins with an index consistently above a defined threshold will be mapped globally. We will then perform a spatial correlation analysis, integrating pre-2026 datasets on land productivity (crop and livestock, following Hoekstra's Kenya study), existing reservoir capacity, and flood occurrence records to classify surplus basins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first global, spatially explicit inventory and typology of chronic water surplus basins, shifting the discourse from pure scarcity to surplus management. It will offer a diagnostic tool for policymakers to prioritize areas for flood mitigation, managed aquifer recharge, or the strategic development of storage to enhance water and land productivity, thereby linking surplus management to food security.
proposals.novelty: While water scarcity is well-studied, systematically defining and mapping 'water surplus basins' as a direct conceptual inversion of scarcity indicators is novel. The integration of this surplus framework with agricultural productivity and storage potential analysis moves beyond flood hazard mapping to a resource-oriented perspective on surplus water.

proposals.title: Moving Baselines for Water Footprint Accounting: Quantifying the Impact of Non-Stationary Hydrology on Livestock and Crop Water Productivity Assessments
proposals.motivation: Water footprint and virtual water flow assessments, foundational to Hoekstra's Water Footprint Network framework, typically rely on long-term average runoff data assuming climatic stationarity. However, observed long-term climate shifts and anthropogenic changes render this 'static baseline' increasingly inaccurate, potentially misrepresenting water scarcity and the true water productivity of agricultural systems. This is critical for informing sustainable resource use in regions like Kenya and the Yellow River Basin, where water for livestock and crops is under pressure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a moving baseline for natural runoff, which accounts for decadal climate trends, will significantly alter calculated water footprints and water productivity metrics for key livestock and crop systems compared to static baseline methods, leading to different conclusions for future resource use scenarios.
proposals.method: We will apply the water footprint assessment framework to case studies in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin, focusing on livestock (e.g., milk production) and major crops. The core innovation is replacing the standard static long-term average (e.g., 1961-1990) with a 30-year moving window baseline (e.g., 1961-1990, 1971-2000, ..., 1991-2020) for calculating blue and green water availability. Water productivity (output per unit water) and land productivity will be recalculated for each moving period. Trends will be analyzed and fed into scenario analyses for future resource use.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological advancement for water footprint accounting under climate non-stationarity, yielding more temporally accurate and policy-relevant water productivity indicators. It will directly improve the robustness of scenario-based projections for agricultural water and land use, a core theme in Hoekstra's recent work.
proposals.novelty: While non-stationarity is acknowledged in climatology, its explicit integration into the operational water footprint accounting framework for agricultural productivity analysis is novel. This moves beyond sensitivity analysis to systematically quantify the bias introduced by the standard static baseline assumption.

proposals.title: Integrating groundwater dynamics into water scarcity footprints: A temporally explicit assessment for livestock and crop production systems
proposals.motivation: Current water scarcity footprint assessments, including the Water Footprint Network's blue water scarcity indicator, often rely on static or annual average renewable surface water availability. This fails to capture the critical dynamics of groundwater systems, where unsustainable depletion is a major threat to food security and ecosystem health, particularly in arid and semi-arid agricultural regions. A temporally explicit integration of groundwater storage change is essential for accurately attributing water scarcity impacts to specific agricultural commodities, such as those studied in Dr. Hoekstra's work on Kenya and the Yellow River Basin.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating monthly groundwater depletion and recharge dynamics into water scarcity footprint calculations will significantly alter the assessed water scarcity impact of key livestock and crop products, revealing hotspots of unsustainable groundwater use that are masked by annual average assessments.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing water scarcity footprint methodology by integrating GRACE (Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment) satellite-derived monthly terrestrial water storage anomaly data (available pre-2026) and groundwater depletion estimates from PCR-GLOBWB or similar global hydrological models. For specific study regions (e.g., key agricultural basins in Kenya and China), we will calculate a revised monthly blue water scarcity indicator that subtracts net groundwater depletion from renewable blue water availability. This dynamic scarcity factor will then be applied to spatially and temporally explicit blue water footprints of livestock (milk, meat) and major crops, using established crop water use models and the MESSAGEix framework for scenario development.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a more physically accurate and temporally dynamic water scarcity footprint indicator, directly enhancing the assessment of water productivity for land and livestock. It will yield novel insights into the contribution of groundwater depletion to the virtual water flows of agricultural trade, supporting more robust scenario analysis for sustainable future resource use under climate change.
proposals.novelty: While groundwater depletion is recognized as a problem, its explicit, temporally resolved integration into product-level water scarcity footprints—bridging satellite-based hydrology with agricultural production analysis—remains a methodological gap. This moves beyond static annual averages to link the timing of groundwater use with the seasonality of crop and livestock production.

proposals.title: Developing a Standardized Water Scarcity Index (SWSI): A probabilistic framework for assessing water stress across sectors and scales
proposals.motivation: Current water scarcity assessments, including the water footprint and scarcity indices, often rely on static annual averages of water use versus availability, failing to capture the probabilistic and temporally variable nature of scarcity. This limits the ability to compare scarcity consistently across different regions, time periods, and economic sectors (e.g., crop, livestock). A standardized, probabilistic index is urgently needed to inform robust scenario analysis for future resource use under climate variability.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the statistical logic of the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) to long-term water footprint and availability data will yield a robust, spatially and temporally comparable Standardized Water Scarcity Index (SWSI). This index will effectively characterize the frequency and intensity of water scarcity events across different sectors.
proposals.method: The method adapts the SPI algorithm, which fits a long-term precipitation record to a probability distribution (e.g., gamma) and transforms it to a standard normal distribution. Here, the input variable will be a monthly or annual time series of the water scarcity index (WSI), defined as the ratio of total blue water footprint to blue water availability for a given basin. We will compute this for major river basins (e.g., the Yellow River Basin) using pre-2026 global datasets. The resulting SWSI values will categorize scarcity events (e.g., SWSI < -2 for extreme scarcity). The analysis will be applied comparatively between agricultural sectors, specifically disaggregating crop and livestock production water use, building on Hoekstra's prior productivity frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first standardized, probabilistic metric for water scarcity that enables direct comparison of scarcity severity across different climates and time scales. It will advance water footprint science by moving from static annual averages to a risk-based assessment, offering a more powerful tool for evaluating resource use scenarios and sustainability in water-intensive sectors like agriculture.
proposals.novelty: While SPI is well-established for meteorological drought, its direct application to create a standardized index for integrated water footprint-based scarcity is novel. This bridges a critical gap between climatological drought indices and socio-economic water use indicators, creating a common statistical language for scarcity.

proposals.title: Integrating satellite-derived soil moisture and groundwater storage into the water footprint accounting framework: A high-resolution assessment for Kenyan livestock systems
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint assessments, including Hoekstra's own work on livestock in Kenya, often rely on coarse hydrological models or climate data for the 'blue water availability' term, introducing significant uncertainty. This limits the accuracy of water scarcity footprint calculations and the reliability of scenarios for future agricultural resource use. The increasing availability of satellite-based hydrological data (pre-2026) offers a transformative opportunity to directly observe key components of the terrestrial water cycle at relevant spatiotemporal scales.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating direct, spatially explicit remote sensing observations of root-zone soil moisture (from missions like SMAP and SMOS) and terrestrial water storage anomalies (from GRACE/GRACE-FO) into the calculation of monthly blue water availability will yield significantly different, and more physically accurate, water scarcity footprints for livestock production in Kenya compared to conventional modeling approaches.
proposals.method: We will use a high-resolution (e.g., 1 km) monthly water balance approach for Kenya, integrating SMAP Level-4 root-zone soil moisture data and GRACE-based groundwater storage anomalies from the JPL Mascons solution to estimate monthly blue water availability. This will be compared against availability derived from the WaterGAP model, a standard in water footprint studies. The new availability term will be integrated into the water footprint accounting framework to compute scarcity-adjusted blue water footprints for major livestock systems (dairy, beef, poultry), following the spatial-explicit methodology of Hoekstra's prior Kenya work. Statistical analysis (e.g., paired t-tests, spatial correlation) will quantify differences.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, observation-based method for quantifying water availability in environmental footprint assessments, moving the field from reliance on models to direct measurement. It will deliver a more robust, spatially detailed assessment of water scarcity pressures associated with Kenyan livestock, directly informing the 'future resource use' scenarios central to Hoekstra's recent papers. The developed methodology will be a blueprint for applying satellite hydrology to footprint studies globally.
proposals.novelty: While remote sensing data is used in hydrology, its direct integration into the operational water footprint accounting framework to replace modeled availability is novel. This approach moves beyond using satellites only for land use or crop classification, applying them to dynamically constrain the denominator of the scarcity footprint equation, a critical innovation for the field.

proposals.title: Inverse Water Footprint Allocation: A Spatial Optimization Framework for Sectoral Re-distribution Under Scarcity Constraints
proposals.motivation: Water footprint accounting has excelled at diagnosing water use but offers limited prescriptive guidance for resource allocation under scarcity. Existing forward models project footprints from given production, but water managers face the inverse problem: given a binding water scarcity constraint, how should water footprints be spatially re-allocated across sectors (e.g., crops, livestock, industry) to maximize economic or nutritional value? This gap hinders the transition from footprint analysis to actionable blueprints for sustainable resource use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solving the inverse allocation problem—optimizing the spatial distribution of sectoral water footprints against a scarcity constraint—will reveal non-intuitive, high-productivity allocation patterns that significantly improve aggregate water productivity compared to current or projected baseline distributions.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatial optimization model building on the Multi-Regional Input-Output (MRIO) and footprint accounting frameworks. The model will treat sectoral water footprints in sub-national units (e.g., Kenyan counties or Yellow River basin provinces) as decision variables. Using linear programming, we will minimize total water scarcity impact (using pre-2026 water stress indices) or maximize total economic/nutritional output (value vs. volume, per Hoekstra's work), subject to constraints on total available water, land suitability, and demand for products. The algorithm will be tested with scenarios varying constraints on trade (virtual water flows) and land use, using pre-2026 data on crop/livestock water and land productivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel decision-support tool that inverts the standard footprint perspective, generating optimal spatial blueprints for water use. It will advance water footprint science from descriptive analysis to prescriptive planning. The results will offer concrete, spatially explicit insights for policymakers on how to re-allocate water resources and production patterns to alleviate scarcity.
proposals.novelty: While footprint studies typically map existing or projected water use, this proposal explicitly solves for the optimal spatial allocation, treating footprints as variables to be optimized—a fundamental shift in perspective. It uniquely integrates the value-versus-volume footprint dichotomy with spatial optimization under joint water-land constraints, a synthesis not yet fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Integrated assessment of virtual water, land, and nitrogen flows in livestock systems: A case study of Kenyan dairy and meat production
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on virtual water flows has highlighted the role of trade in redistributing water scarcity, but land and nutrient impacts are often assessed in isolation. For livestock systems, which are major drivers of land use change and nitrogen pollution, a singular focus on water may lead to inefficient or unsustainable resource management recommendations. A simultaneous analysis of these three critical footprints is needed to identify potential trade-offs and synergies in agricultural trade and productivity strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for Kenyan livestock production, the spatial patterns and resource efficiency trade-offs between virtual water, land, and nitrogen flows are significant, and that optimizing for one resource (e.g., water productivity) may inadvertently increase pressure on others (e.g., nitrogen leaching or land expansion).
proposals.method: Building on Hoekstra's prior Kenyan livestock productivity study, we will extend the analysis using a spatially explicit Material Flow Analysis (MFA) framework. For defined livestock systems (dairy, beef, poultry), we will quantify the virtual water, land, and nitrogen footprints per unit of product (milk, meat) across different production zones. Using trade flow data and footprint multipliers, we will map inter-regional virtual flows within Kenya. The analysis will employ scenario-based modeling to compare future development pathways, assessing how shifts in productivity, feed sourcing, and trade alter the balance and coupling of the three resource flows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated accounting of virtual water, land, and nitrogen flows for a national livestock sector, moving beyond single-footprint studies. It will generate concrete metrics on resource coupling and trade-offs, offering a more holistic evidence base for policies targeting sustainable intensification and circular bioeconomy principles in agriculture.
proposals.novelty: While virtual water and land studies exist, their concurrent analysis with embedded nutrient flows (particularly nitrogen) in livestock systems is novel. The explicit focus on the 'dual problem'—the potential divergence in optimization pathways for different embedded resources—represents a significant conceptual and methodological advance in footprint and trade analysis.

proposals.title: Advancing Water Footprint Reliability: A Framework for Calibrating and Quantifying Uncertainty in Agricultural Water Balance Models
proposals.motivation: Water footprint and virtual water flow assessments, such as those for livestock and crops, rely on water balance models that are often applied with fixed, literature-based parameters, lacking formal calibration and uncertainty analysis. This limits the robustness of scenarios for future resource use, particularly in data-scarce regions critical to global food-water-land nexus studies. Integrating established hydrological calibration techniques can significantly improve the credibility of these assessments for policy and management.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying formal parameter estimation and uncertainty quantification techniques from complex hydrological models (e.g., SWAT, HBV) to the water balance model underlying the Water Footprint Assessment framework will yield more reliable and spatially differentiated estimates of green and blue water consumption for key agricultural commodities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation (GLUE) methodology and the Soil and Water Assessment Tool (SWAT) calibration protocol (SWAT-CUP) for use with the crop water requirement and soil water balance calculations central to water footprint accounting. The approach will involve: 1) identifying sensitive parameters (e.g., crop coefficients, soil water holding capacity) in the footprint model; 2) defining likelihood measures based on observed data (e.g., crop yields, evapotranspiration, river basin discharge); and 3) performing Monte Carlo simulations to generate posterior parameter distributions and uncertainty bounds for water footprints of selected livestock (e.g., dairy) and crop systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological advancement for the Water Footprint Network community, producing the first formally calibrated and uncertainty-aware water footprint estimates for agricultural production. It will directly enhance the scenarios analyzed in Hoekstra's recent work on Kenya and the Yellow River Basin by providing probabilistic ranges for future water and land productivity projections, thereby strengthening the evidence base for sustainable resource use strategies.
proposals.novelty: While parameter calibration and uncertainty analysis are standard in process-based hydrology, their systematic application to the simpler, but widely used, water balance model of Water Footprint Assessment is novel. This bridges a critical methodological gap between detailed hydrological modeling and applied environmental footprint accounting.

proposals.title: A Near-Real-Time Water Footprint Dashboard for Agricultural Production: Integrating Meteorological Data Streams into the Water Footprint Assessment Framework
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint assessments are predominantly retrospective, using historical climate and production data. This limits their utility for proactive water resource management and policy-making in the face of increasing hydrological variability. A dynamic, near-real-time monitoring system is needed to translate the foundational Water Footprint concept into an operational tool for stakeholders.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating high-frequency meteorological data streams into the crop and livestock water footprint models will enable the creation of a temporally scaled, operational dashboard that provides actionable insights into current water productivity and resource stress, surpassing the informational value of static annual assessments.
proposals.method: The dashboard will be built on a cloud-based architecture (e.g., AWS or Google Cloud) that ingests daily meteorological data (precipitation, temperature, reference evapotranspiration) from sources like NASA POWER or ERA5. The core algorithm will implement the FAO-56 Penman-Monteith method for crop water requirement calculations, updated daily, and integrate these with spatially explicit data on crop areas and livestock distributions (following Hoekstra's Kenyan study methodology). The system will compute and visualize daily blue and green water footprints for key commodities, presenting trends and anomalies via an interactive web interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will operationalize the water footprint concept, transitioning it from a accounting metric to a monitoring tool. It will provide a prototype for national or basin-scale water productivity surveillance, offering farmers, policymakers, and supply chain managers timely data to inform irrigation decisions, drought response, and sustainable procurement strategies.
proposals.novelty: While water footprint modeling is established, its application in a near-real-time, dashboard format using live data feeds is novel. This represents a significant temporal scaling of the assessment framework, moving from annual resolution to sub-seasonal monitoring, which has not been implemented at a systemic level pre-2026.

proposals.title: Spatially Explicit Nitrogen Leaching Fractions for Improved Water Footprint and Land Productivity Assessment in Livestock Systems
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and land productivity assessments for livestock and crop systems, as exemplified by Hoekstra's work in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin, often rely on uniform nitrogen leaching fractions. This oversimplification ignores critical spatial heterogeneity in soil properties, climate, and farm management, leading to inaccurate estimates of grey water footprints and misinformed resource use scenarios. A spatially variable approach is essential for robust policy and investment decisions regarding sustainable intensification of agriculture.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating spatially variable nitrogen leaching fractions, derived from soil type, climate, and management practice data, will significantly alter the calculated grey water footprint and land productivity indicators for key livestock and crop systems compared to assessments using a uniform leaching fraction.
proposals.method: We will apply the Soil and Water Assessment Tool (SWAT) or a similar process-based hydrological model, calibrated with pre-2026 regional data, to simulate nitrate leaching across different soil-climate zones for major feed crops (e.g., maize) and pasture systems. The simulated leaching fractions will be integrated into a revised water footprint calculation framework. This analysis will be conducted for a case study region (e.g., a major agricultural basin in East Africa or China) to create high-resolution maps of livestock and crop water footprints, which will then be compared to results from the standard uniform-fraction method.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological advancement for water footprint accounting by demonstrating a practical pathway to spatialize the grey water component. It will yield more accurate and location-specific assessments of water pollution associated with food production, directly enhancing the scenarios used for future resource use planning in livestock-dense regions.
proposals.novelty: While the importance of spatial detail is acknowledged, pre-2026 water footprint studies have not systematically operationalized variable nitrogen leaching fractions at a basin or national scale for integrated livestock-crop systems, moving from a conceptual critique to a modeled, scenario-based assessment.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating water and land footprints of derived crop products: A systematic comparison of LCA allocation methods versus mass/economic allocation
proposals.motivation: Water footprint and virtual water flow assessments for processed crop products (e.g., oil from seeds, flour from grains) commonly rely on mass or economic allocation, which can misrepresent the environmental burden associated with specific co-products. This is a critical gap, as these allocation choices directly influence sustainability metrics for agricultural systems and trade, a core theme in Hoekstra's work. With increasing pressure on water and land resources, more robust and physically grounded allocation methods are needed to inform policy and resource management.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying ISO-conformant Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) allocation procedures (e.g., system expansion, physical causality-based allocation) to derived crop products will yield significantly different, and more environmentally justified, water and land productivity indicators compared to the standard mass or economic allocation methods currently prevalent in water footprint studies.
proposals.method: The research will conduct a comparative case study analysis for major crop processing chains (e.g., wheat to flour/bran, soybeans to oil/cake, olives to oil/pomace). For each chain, we will calculate water footprints (blue, green, grey) and land footprints using: (1) mass allocation, (2) economic allocation, and (3) relevant LCA procedures (system expansion/substitution and physical allocation based on causal relationships, such as energy content or biochemical composition). The analysis will utilize existing data on crop water use, yields, and processing efficiencies from pre-2026 literature and databases, applying them within the computational framework of the Water Footprint Assessment manual.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological bridge between LCA and water footprint assessment, offering a standardized, reproducible approach for allocating resource use to agricultural co-products. It will deliver a critical evaluation of how allocation choice affects the perceived efficiency and sustainability of crop production systems, thereby improving the accuracy of virtual water flow calculations and scenarios for future resource use.
proposals.novelty: While LCA allocation is established in carbon footprinting, its systematic application and comparison to standard methods for water and land footprints of derived crop products is novel. This directly extends Hoekstra's recent work on livestock systems in Kenya and crop value in the Yellow River Basin by introducing a more nuanced allocation framework from industrial ecology.

proposals.title: Dynamic Grey Water Footprint Assessment in River Basins: Integrating Hydrological Models for Pollutant Dilution Capacity Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current grey water footprint (GWF) accounting, as defined by the Water Footprint Network, uses a static approach based on ambient water quality standards and a generic dilution factor. This fails to capture the spatio-temporal variability of a river's actual assimilation capacity, which depends on dynamic hydrological conditions. In water-scarce and polluted basins, this oversimplification can lead to inaccurate assessments of water sustainability for agricultural and livestock production systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating dynamic, process-based hydrological models into GWF accounting will yield significantly different and more spatially and temporally resolved estimates of water pollution impacts compared to the current static method, revealing critical periods and locations of unsustainable water use.
proposals.method: We will employ the Soil and Water Assessment Tool (SWAT), a pre-2026 established river basin-scale model, to simulate daily streamflow and pollutant transport (focusing on nitrogen and phosphorus from fertilizers and livestock waste) for a case study basin (e.g., the Tana River Basin in Kenya). The model's simulated flow volumes will be used to dynamically calculate the dilution capacity required to assimilate pollutant loads from crop and livestock production, following the GWF framework. Scenarios of changing land use and agricultural productivity will be analyzed to project future grey water demands against climate-influenced dilution capacities.
proposals.contribution: This work will advance the grey water footprint methodology from a static to a dynamic, physically-based assessment tool. It will provide basin planners with high-resolution data on when and where pollutant loads exceed the river's dilution capacity, directly linking agricultural production metrics to tangible water quality constraints. The results will offer more robust evidence for prioritizing pollution mitigation in river basin management.
proposals.novelty: While SWAT is commonly used for pollution modeling and the GWF is a known indicator, their integration to dynamically quantify the dilution capacity *for the specific purpose of grey water footprinting* represents a novel synthesis. This moves beyond applying a single dilution factor to using the model's simulated hydrology as the direct basis for the footprint calculation.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model of Agricultural Trade Decisions to Simulate Emergent Virtual Water Flows in Kenya Under Climate and Policy Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Current assessments of virtual water flows often rely on top-down, equilibrium-based models, which may not capture the heterogeneous and adaptive decision-making of farmers and traders. This limits our ability to project how resource use efficiency and water security might evolve under dynamic stressors like climate variability and shifting agricultural policies, particularly in data-scarce regions like Kenya.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simulating the bounded-rational decisions of heterogeneous agents (farmers, traders) will reveal emergent virtual water flow patterns that differ significantly from projections of aggregated, optimization-based models, especially under scenarios of water pricing and climate-induced yield variability.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a structure similar to the ODD protocol. Farmer agents, parameterized with data from Hoekstra's prior work on Kenyan livestock and crop water productivity, will make land-use and production decisions based on profit, risk aversion, and local water stress. Trader agents will facilitate inter-regional trade based on price differentials and transaction costs. The model will be implemented in NetLogo or a similar ABM platform, with decision rules informed by behavioral economics. Scenarios will test combinations of climate projections (from IPCC AR5) and policies (e.g., subsidies, water quotas).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, bottom-up framework for analyzing the resilience and efficiency of water resource systems through trade. It will generate spatially explicit insights into how micro-level decisions aggregate to shape macro-level virtual water flows, offering policymakers a tool to anticipate unintended consequences of interventions on water and land use.
proposals.novelty: While Hoekstra's work has quantified water footprints and productivities, applying agent-based modeling to simulate the decision processes driving virtual water trade in an integrated crop-livestock system is a novel methodological advance for the water footprint community.

proposals.title: A Global Multi-Regional Input-Output Model for Comprehensive Water Footprint Accounting: Integrating Green, Blue, and Grey Water Flows
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint assessments and virtual water trade studies are often limited to specific regions, single water types, or aggregated sectors, failing to capture the complex, global interdependencies of water use in production and consumption. As water scarcity intensifies globally, policymakers need a consistent, high-resolution tool to trace the embodied water in international supply chains and inform equitable and efficient resource management strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating spatially explicit green, blue, and grey water footprint data into a global Multi-Regional Input-Output (MRIO) framework will reveal significantly different patterns of virtual water flows and dependencies compared to analyses using only blue water or single-region models, particularly for water-intensive agricultural and livestock sectors.
proposals.method: We will construct a global MRIO model using the latest pre-2026 EXIOBASE or GTAP database as the economic core. Country- and sector-specific green, blue, and grey water footprint coefficients (m³/unit output) will be calculated using the Water Footprint Assessment methodology, drawing on FAO and Mekonnen & Hoekstra datasets. These coefficients will be linked to the MRIO structure using Leontief inverse analysis to compute virtual water flows. The model will be validated against known virtual water trade estimates for major crops and livestock products from prior studies.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first globally consistent, sector-detailed MRIO database that fully disaggregates green, blue, and grey water footprints for all countries. It will enable analysis of how international trade redistributes different types of water scarcity and pollution, offering a robust evidence base for evaluating consumption-based water responsibility and sustainable resource use scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While global MRIOs exist for blue water, the systematic integration of all three water footprint components (green, blue, grey) at a full sectoral and country resolution represents a significant advancement, allowing for a nuanced assessment of land productivity (green water) and pollution (grey water) alongside consumptive use (blue water) in a unified trade model.

proposals.title: Optimizing Global Diets for Planetary Water Boundaries: An Inverse Analysis of Nutrition Security under Blue and Grey Water Constraints
proposals.motivation: Global freshwater scarcity is intensifying, with agriculture accounting for the majority of blue and grey water use. Existing water footprint assessments describe current impacts but do not prescribe forward-looking, sustainable dietary pathways. This research addresses the critical inverse problem: defining nutritionally adequate diets that collectively operate within sustainable global water withdrawal and pollution budgets, a necessity for long-term water and food security.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying optimization techniques to global crop and livestock water productivity data, it is possible to derive nutritionally complete human diets that respect defined planetary blue and grey water boundaries, and that these optimal diets will necessitate significant shifts from current global average consumption patterns, particularly for animal-source foods.
proposals.method: We will employ linear programming to solve the inverse optimization problem. The objective function will minimize the deviation from current global average diets, subject to constraints: 1) meeting nutritional requirements (energy, protein, key micronutrients) for a global population, 2) staying within global blue water availability (considering environmental flow requirements) and assimilative capacity for grey water (nitrogen), and 3) respecting regional production potentials. The model will be built using spatially explicit water footprint databases (e.g., WaterStat, Mekonnen & Hoekstra) and FAO food balance sheets and nutrition data, creating scenarios for different water constraint levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first globally comprehensive, quantitative framework for defining water-sustainable dietary targets, moving beyond footprint accounting to solution-oriented planning. It will yield concrete dietary baskets and quantify the required reductions in water-intensive food items, offering a science-based benchmark for policies linking water resource management, agricultural planning, and dietary guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While Hoekstra's prior work quantified livestock and crop water productivity, this study inverts the logic to solve for the diet given the resource constraint, a novel application of optimization to the water footprint concept. It uniquely integrates both blue and grey water boundaries simultaneously into a global dietary optimization model.

proposals.title: Water Footprint Scenarios for Protein Transition Pathways: A Comparative Analysis of Livestock, Crops, and Novel Alternatives
proposals.motivation: Global dietary shifts towards more plant-based and alternative proteins are proposed to improve food security and reduce environmental impacts. However, the water resource implications of such a transition are not fully quantified at a systemic level, creating a critical knowledge gap for sustainable resource planning. This research addresses this gap by applying the water footprint assessment framework, a core pre-2026 methodology, to model the water consequences of different protein futures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transition from animal-based to plant-based and novel alternative proteins will significantly reduce the consumptive blue and green water footprint per unit of dietary protein, but that the magnitude of savings and spatial distribution of water use will vary substantially depending on the specific protein sources and production systems considered.
proposals.method: We will develop spatially explicit scenarios for protein production in a major river basin (e.g., the Yellow River Basin, building on prior work). The method will calculate and compare the green, blue, and grey water footprints per gram of protein for key livestock (beef, dairy, poultry) and crop (pulses, cereals) products using established Water Footprint Network databases and algorithms. Novel protein sources (e.g., cultured meat precursors, insect meal) will be included based on pre-2026 life cycle inventory data. Scenarios will model different substitution pathways (e.g., beef replaced by legumes vs. poultry vs. alternatives) and aggregate impacts on total water use.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, scenario-based quantification of water footprint trade-offs in the protein transition, moving beyond simple crop-livestock comparisons. It will deliver a robust evidence base for policymakers and agri-food stakeholders to navigate water sustainability in dietary change. The findings will advance the field of footprint scenario analysis by integrating novel protein pathways into established assessment frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While water footprints of individual foods are known, the novelty lies in modeling complete dietary transition *pathways* and their aggregate water impacts at the basin scale, explicitly incorporating emerging alternative proteins into the scenario framework alongside conventional livestock and crops.

proposals.title: Dynamic Sustainability Thresholds for Livestock Systems: Integrating Technological Innovation and Ecosystem Dynamics in Water and Land Use Scenarios
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and land productivity assessments, including our own work in Kenya, often rely on static thresholds for sustainable resource use (e.g., maximum water availability per basin). These static benchmarks fail to account for the dynamic interplay of technological improvements in feed crops and livestock management, and potential ecosystem recovery under altered management. This limits the realism of long-term scenarios for sustainable agricultural intensification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling water and land sustainability limits as dynamic functions of technological change rates and ecosystem recovery potentials will yield significantly different, and more attainable, long-term scenarios for livestock production compared to scenarios based on static thresholds.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic system dynamics model, building on our previous livestock productivity frameworks for Kenya. The model will integrate: 1) a technological innovation module altering crop water productivity and feed conversion efficiency over time based on historical diffusion curves; 2) an ecosystem recovery module where land and water regeneration rates are functions of reduced pressure from improved productivity. We will run comparative scenario analyses (e.g., business-as-usual vs. accelerated innovation) to project future water and land footprints, contrasting outcomes from dynamic versus static sustainability ceilings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework for assessing sustainable production limits that are responsive to human and natural system feedbacks. It will offer more nuanced policy insights for regions like Kenya, showing how investment in technology can effectively 'raise' the ceiling for sustainable livestock production, complementing our earlier work on volume versus value in water footprints.
proposals.novelty: While scenario analysis is common, explicitly modeling the sustainability threshold itself as an endogenous, dynamic variable—co-evolving with productivity and ecosystem state—represents a significant conceptual and computational advance beyond the prevailing paradigm of fixed planetary or regional boundaries.

proposals.title: A Sectoral Benchmarking Framework for Water and Land Productivity: Integrating Physical and Economic Efficiency Metrics
proposals.motivation: Current resource footprint assessments, such as water and land footprints, often operate in sectoral silos, making it difficult to compare efficiency across different economic activities like crop production, livestock, and industrial manufacturing. This hinders the identification of strategic priorities for sustainable resource allocation at national and global scales. A standardized, cross-sectoral benchmarking system is urgently needed to inform coherent policies for the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly those related to water (SDG 6), responsible consumption (SDG 12), and life on land (SDG 15).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating physical resource productivity (e.g., m³ water per ton of product) with economic resource productivity (e.g., m³ water per unit of economic value added) will reveal distinct and actionable efficiency benchmarks for different sectors and nations, challenging assumptions based on single-metric analyses.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-metric life cycle assessment framework, building upon the Water Footprint Network methodology. For key sectors (e.g., staple crops, dairy/beef livestock, textiles, thermal power), we will calculate standardized physical water and land productivity ratios (output/input). These will be integrated with economic value-added data from global trade databases (e.g., GTAP) to compute economic productivity ratios. A novel composite benchmarking index will be developed using Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) to rank sector-nation combinations, controlling for climatic and developmental factors. Scenario analysis will project efficiency gains under different technological and dietary change pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first globally applicable, sector-transcendent benchmarking protocol for resource use efficiency, moving beyond descriptive footprint accounting. It will deliver a publicly accessible database of sectoral benchmarks, offering policymakers and investors a tool to identify 'hotspots' for improvement and trade-offs between physical yield optimization and economic resource valorization.
proposals.novelty: While sector-specific footprint studies exist, the systematic integration of physical and economic productivity into a single benchmarking index for cross-sector comparison is novel. The application of DEA to harmonize these dual efficiency perspectives across diverse industries—from agriculture to manufacturing—represents a significant methodological advance in resource footprint science.

proposals.title: Integrating Economic Input-Output Analysis with Physical Accounting to Quantify Sectoral Water and Land Footprints in Kenya
proposals.motivation: Current water and land footprint assessments, such as those for livestock in Kenya, often rely on process-based life cycle assessment (LCA) or bottom-up sectoral analyses, which can miss complex inter-sectoral dependencies and economic drivers of resource use. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need to link physical resource productivity metrics (e.g., m³ water/kg milk) with the economic structure of a national economy to better inform integrated resource policy and scenario planning.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting national economic input-output (IO) tables to incorporate sector-specific water and land use coefficients, we can more accurately and comprehensively trace the virtual water and land embodied in final consumption and trade, revealing critical, economically-driven resource hotspots that are obscured in conventional footprint analyses.
proposals.method: We will construct a hybrid environmentally extended input-output (EEIO) model for Kenya, integrating physical data on livestock and crop water/land productivity from Hoekstra's prior work with the latest available national IO table (e.g., from KNBS). The core algorithm will involve the Leontief inverse matrix to calculate total (direct and indirect) resource requirements per monetary unit of sectoral output. Sectoral footprints will be allocated to final demand categories, enabling analysis of trade-offs between 'volume versus value' for different agricultural and industrial commodities.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, economy-wide mapping of water and land footprints across Kenyan sectors, quantifying the indirect resource use triggered by inter-industry transactions. It will offer a robust analytical framework for developing future resource-use scenarios that are consistent with both biophysical constraints and economic development pathways, directly extending the livestock-focused productivity studies.
proposals.novelty: While EEIO is established in carbon footprinting, its application to spatially explicit water and land footprints in a data-scarce, agriculturally-driven African economy like Kenya is novel. It moves beyond analyzing individual livestock or crop systems to model the entire resource network shaped by economic interdependence.

proposals.title: From Individual Choices to National Footprints: An Agent-Based Model of Consumer Behavior for Water and Land Resource Accounting
proposals.motivation: Current water and land footprint assessments, including our own work on livestock and crops, rely on top-down, aggregate data and static scenarios. This masks the critical role of heterogeneous consumer behavior, socioeconomic trends, and feedback loops in shaping resource demand. Understanding the micro-foundations of consumption is essential for designing effective and equitable policies to reduce future resource pressure, particularly in rapidly developing regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the aggregation of heterogeneous, socially influenced consumer choices leads to national water and land footprints that are non-linear and systematically different from projections based on average per capita consumption or homogeneous population groups.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) using a framework like NetLogo or Mesa. Agents (consumers/households) will be parameterized with attributes (income, location, cultural preferences) derived from pre-2026 household survey data (e.g., for Kenya). Decision rules for food and goods consumption will incorporate theory from behavioral economics (bounded rationality, social norms). The model will simulate interactions and choices over time, scaling agent decisions to national footprints using detailed product-specific water and land footprint databases from the Water Footprint Network. Calibration and validation will use historical footprint data.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, bottom-up methodology for footprint analysis, moving beyond static averages to capture behavioral dynamics. It will generate new insights into how inequality, social diffusion, and policy interventions (e.g., information campaigns, pricing) propagate through a population to affect aggregate resource use, thereby offering a more robust tool for scenario development.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs are used in other fields, their application to integrate detailed, spatially explicit water and land footprint accounting with empirically grounded models of consumer choice represents a significant methodological advance for the footprint community, bridging the gap between micro-scale behavior and macro-scale resource metrics.

proposals.title: Ecological Network Analysis of Virtual Water Flows: Identifying Critical Nodes and Resilience in Agricultural Production Systems
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and virtual water flow assessments, such as those for crop and livestock systems, quantify volumes but lack a systemic understanding of the network's structure and vulnerabilities. This limits the ability to predict how shocks in one part of a water system (e.g., a drought in a key crop-producing region) cascade through trade networks, impacting food and water security. A network-based resilience analysis is urgently needed to inform sustainable resource allocation and trade policies.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying ecological network analysis (ENA) to virtual water flow networks will reveal non-obvious critical nodes (sectors or regions) whose disruption disproportionately reduces system-wide efficiency and resilience. We hypothesize that the land and water productivity of these nodes, as studied in Hoekstra's prior work, is a key but insufficient predictor of their systemic criticality.
proposals.method: We will adapt established ENA metrics (e.g., ascendency, redundancy, node centrality) to model virtual water flow networks. Using multi-regional input-output data and water footprint accounts (e.g., for crops, milk, and livestock), we will construct directed, weighted networks of water embodied in trade. The analysis will employ algorithms for calculating network flow-based centrality (e.g., eigenvector centrality) and will conduct scenario-based node removal simulations to test resilience and identify bottlenecks. The framework will be applied to a case study, such as the Yellow River Basin or Kenya, integrating local productivity data.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, systems-level diagnostic tool for water management, moving beyond volumetric accounting to functional network analysis. It will generate maps of critical dependencies and resilience hotspots within agricultural water systems, offering concrete insights for prioritizing interventions to secure water and food supply chains under global change.
proposals.novelty: While ENA is established in ecology, its application to engineered-human-hydrological systems like virtual water networks is nascent. This work innovatively merges Hoekstra's water footprint and productivity assessment framework with rigorous network ecology methods to uncover systemic properties not visible through footprint volumes alone.

proposals.title: Designing for Zero: A Scenario-Based Inverse Modeling Framework for Net-Zero Blue and Grey Water Footprints in Mixed Crop-Livestock Systems
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint assessments are largely descriptive, quantifying impacts but not prescribing solutions. Given increasing water scarcity and pollution, there is an urgent need to shift from impact accounting to sustainable system design. This research addresses the critical gap in methods to proactively engineer agricultural systems that achieve net-zero blue water consumption and grey water pollution, aligning with pre-2026 calls for transformative water stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the standard water footprint calculation, it is possible to identify feasible combinations of crop patterns, livestock productivity, and management practices that achieve a net-zero blue and grey water footprint at a system scale, without compromising economic output.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse modeling framework building on Hoekstra's Water Footprint Assessment methodology. For a case study region (e.g., a Kenyan catchment), we will first establish a baseline using local data on crop and livestock water productivity (m³/ton, m³/kg protein). The core algorithm will treat target water footprints (zero blue/grey) as constraints and solve for optimal system configurations. This involves linear programming to explore scenarios varying: (1) crop mix and yields, (2) livestock feed sourcing and milk/meat productivity, and (3) irrigation technology and nitrogen application rates. Sensitivity analysis will identify key leverage points (e.g., feed conversion efficiency, fertilizer use efficiency) for achieving the zero-footprint target.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable planning tool for policymakers and agricultural planners to design water-sustainable food systems. It will advance water footprint science from accounting to prescriptive analytics. The scenario outputs will quantify the necessary trade-offs and synergies between land use, livestock management, and water resources to meet stringent water sustainability goals.
proposals.novelty: While forward calculations of water footprints are common, the explicit inversion of the model to design for a zero target is a novel, solution-oriented approach. It uniquely integrates crop and livestock systems within a single optimization framework to solve for a combined water quality and quantity objective, moving beyond incremental efficiency gains.

proposals.title: Assimilating heterogeneous data into a parsimonious process model to quantify dynamic nitrogen leaching and its uncertainty for agricultural systems
proposals.motivation: Nitrogen leaching from agriculture is a primary cause of water pollution and eutrophication, yet its quantification remains highly uncertain due to spatial heterogeneity and data scarcity. Existing models are either overly simplistic (e.g., fixed leaching fractions) or too complex and data-hungry for operational use, limiting reliable scenario analysis for sustainable intensification. A robust, data-informed model is needed to provide actionable, confidence-bounded estimates of nitrogen losses under different land and water management strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid model, combining a parsimonious process-based core with a flexible data assimilation framework, can dynamically integrate disparate real-world data streams to significantly reduce parametric uncertainty and produce more reliable, confidence-interval-bounded estimates of nitrogen leaching than static or complex unconstrained models.
