proposals.method: We will develop a core nitrogen mass balance model, parameterized for key crops and livestock systems, informed by Hoekstra's work on water and land productivity. This will be coupled with an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) to assimilate time-series data from satellite-derived vegetation indices, soil moisture, and sporadic farm-level nitrogen application and yield records. The EnKF will continuously update model states and parameters, with ensemble spread providing quantitative prediction intervals for leaching fluxes under different management scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver an operational, testable model framework that explicitly quantifies uncertainty in nitrogen leaching, moving beyond point estimates. It will provide a methodological advance in fusing process understanding with heterogeneous data, offering a practical tool for assessing the water quality implications of future agricultural resource use and productivity pathways.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific fusion of a parsimonious, scalable process model designed for agricultural systems with a robust data assimilation scheme tailored for sparse, multi-source data, directly producing operational confidence intervals for dynamic leaching—a gap between simplistic and overly complex modeling approaches.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Reservoir Network Model for Nitrogen Leaching: Moving Beyond Constant Leaching Fractions in Agricultural Water Footprint and Land Productivity Assessments
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and land productivity assessments, including those for livestock and crop systems, often rely on static nitrogen leaching fractions, a major source of uncertainty. This oversimplification fails to capture the dynamic response of leaching to climate variability and management practices, undermining the accuracy of resource use projections and scenario analyses. A more process-based approach is needed to robustly link agricultural production to its environmental impacts on water quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conceptualizing the soil nitrogen cycle as a network of interconnected, state-dependent reservoirs will yield a more accurate and spatially/temporally explicit simulation of nitrogen leaching than models using constant leaching fractions. This dynamic representation will better explain observed variations in leaching across different climates, soils, and management regimes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the reservoir network concept from rainfall-runoff hydrology (e.g., HBV model concepts) to nitrogen cycling. The soil profile will be discretized into linked reservoirs representing mineral N (ammonium, nitrate), immobilized N, and subsoil N. Fluxes between reservoirs (mineralization, nitrification, plant uptake, leaching) will be governed by transfer functions parameterized by soil properties (texture, organic matter), driven by daily climate data (precipitation, temperature, evapotranspiration), and forced by management inputs (fertilizer type, timing, irrigation). The model will be implemented in Python, calibrated and validated using existing field-scale leaching datasets from contrasting agricultural systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, transferable modeling framework to dynamically quantify nitrogen leaching as a function of system state, directly improving the assessment of agricultural water pollution in water footprint studies. It will enable more reliable scenario analyses for future resource use, particularly in evaluating trade-offs between livestock/crop productivity and water quality under changing management and climate.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual analogy from dynamic watershed hydrology to the soil nitrogen cycle, replacing a static environmental impact coefficient with a process-based, state-variable model. This integrates biogeochemical cycling with hydrological drivers in a way not commonly applied in water footprint and resource productivity accounting pre-2026.

proposals.title: Co-evolutionary Pathways: An Agent-Based Model of Adaptive Crop-Livestock Systems and Water Resource Dynamics in Data-Scarce Basins
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and land productivity assessments, while foundational, often treat water availability as a static boundary condition. In reality, agricultural water use (blue and green) and land use decisions are dynamically coupled, especially under climate variability. This gap limits the ability to project how farmer adaptations and policy interventions might reshape both agricultural landscapes and long-term water resource sustainability, a critical issue for food security.
proposals.hypothesis: Simulating heterogeneous farmer agents who adaptively co-evolve their crop-livestock choices and irrigation strategies with dynamically updated water constraints will reveal emergent, sustainable system equilibria that are not identifiable through top-down, static water allocation models.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) where farmer agents, characterized by resource endowments (land, capital), make annual decisions on crop mix (including fodder for livestock), irrigation technology, and water use based on spatio-temporal profiles of green/blue water, market signals, and observed neighbor success. The core innovation is a bidirectional feedback loop: agent decisions alter blue water demand and groundwater recharge, which dynamically updates the water constraint module for the next season using a simplified soil-water balance. The model will be parameterized and tested for a Kenyan case study, leveraging Hoekstra's prior work on livestock water and land productivity in Kenya.
proposals.contribution: This work will advance the water footprint methodology by integrating it into a dynamic, behavioral simulation framework. It will provide a new tool for exploring how bottom-up adaptations influence the long-term viability of water resources and agricultural productivity, offering insights for policies on water allocation, agricultural extension, and climate adaptation in coupled human-water systems.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the traditional water planning paradigm by starting from dynamic water constraints to simulate emergent land use, rather than allocating water to predefined demands, and explicitly models the closed feedback loop between agricultural decisions and the renewability of the water resource base.

proposals.title: Disaggregating Agricultural Footprints: A Sub-Grid Unmixing Framework for Water and Land Productivity Assessment
proposals.motivation: Global water and land footprint assessments, such as those in the Water Footprint Network framework, often rely on coarse (e.g., 5-arc-minute) spatial data, assuming homogeneous conditions within grid cells. This obscures critical sub-grid heterogeneity in soil type, topography, and farm management, leading to significant uncertainty in productivity estimates and resource use scenarios, particularly for mixed crop-livestock systems as studied in Kenya. Refining these estimates is essential for targeting sustainable intensification and equitable resource policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting spectral mixture analysis (SMA) principles to decompose coarse agricultural grid cells into functional endmembers (e.g., distinct soil-slope-farm size classes) will yield more accurate and spatially explicit estimates of water consumption and land productivity than standard homogeneous-cell models.
proposals.method: We will define a set of discrete agricultural endmembers (e.g., 'clay soil, gentle slope, large farm') using pre-2026 global datasets (SoilGrids, SRTM topography, and field size data from the Global Field Size map). For each 5-arc-minute cell, we will calculate the fractional area of each endmember via a linear unmixing algorithm, constraining fractions to sum to one. Water balances and footprint intensities (m³/ton, kg/ha) will be computed per endmember using localized crop/livestock parameters and climate data, then aggregated area-weighted to the grid cell level, creating a new high-resolution footprint product.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological framework to quantify and map the sub-grid variability of water and land footprints, directly enhancing the spatial accuracy of large-scale agricultural models. It will yield improved scenario analyses for future resource use, offering policymakers insights into the localized drivers of productivity gaps and resource efficiency in systems like those in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual translation of a remote sensing solution (spectral unmixing) to a distinct environmental modeling problem (agricultural footprint heterogeneity), creating a 'grid cell unmixing' paradigm that explicitly accounts for internal variability ignored by standard aggregated approaches.

proposals.title: Mapping the Grey Water Footprint to Ecological Carrying Capacity: A Spatially Explicit Model for Pollution Assimilation in River Basins
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint assessments, including the grey water footprint, rely on fixed ambient water quality standards, which ignore the natural spatial variability in a water body's capacity to assimilate pollutants. This leads to inefficient water resource management and can either over-protect or under-protect aquatic ecosystems. Adapting the ecological carrying capacity concept from wildlife and livestock management offers a pathway to more scientifically grounded, spatially differentiated water pollution regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: The natural pollution assimilation capacity of a river segment can be quantified as a dynamic function of local hydrological, biological, and geochemical conditions. Spatially explicit modeling of this capacity will reveal significant heterogeneity within basins, enabling more accurate and protective grey water footprint allocations than current standard-based methods.
proposals.method: The research will develop a process-based, spatially distributed model building on pre-2026 hydrological and water quality modeling frameworks (e.g., SWAT, QUAL2K). For key pollutants (e.g., nitrogen, phosphorus), the model will integrate gridded data on flow velocity, temperature, dissolved oxygen, sediment load, and microbial activity to calculate local decay rates. Using river network routing algorithms, these local capacities will be aggregated to determine sustainable pollutant load thresholds for each catchment, creating a 'carrying capacity map' analogous to spatial habitat models in ecology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology to calculate a spatially differentiated grey water footprint, moving beyond static dilution assumptions. It will deliver a practical modeling framework for regulatory agencies to set discharge permits based on local ecosystem resilience, directly linking the water footprint concept to physical environmental limits.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly translating the 'carrying capacity' framework from terrestrial ecology and livestock productivity studies to the domain of water pollution regulation. It replaces a universal concentration standard with a spatially variable, process-driven capacity model, a significant shift in both footprint accounting and water quality management philosophy.

proposals.title: Modeling Cascading Farmer Decisions in Water Use: An Agent-Based Network Approach for the Yellow River Basin
proposals.motivation: Understanding the dynamics of agricultural water use is critical for sustainable resource management, especially in water-scarce basins like the Yellow River. Current water footprint and productivity assessments often rely on aggregate or equilibrium models, which fail to capture the non-linear, cascading effects of individual farmer decisions driven by social influence and physical constraints. This gap limits the ability to predict how shocks or policies propagate through a farming community, potentially leading to tipping points in regional blue water depletion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the adoption of irrigation strategies among farmers follows a complex contagion process within dual social-physical networks, and that this process creates identifiable, non-linear thresholds in regional water use that are missed by conventional models.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) where farmers are nodes in two overlapping networks: a social network for information exchange and a physical network of shared water infrastructure. Farmer states (e.g., water-intensive, water-saving, fallow) will transition based on rules adapted from epidemiological SIR models, incorporating local water availability (from hydrological data), perceived economic risk, and the observed states of network neighbors. The model will be parameterized and validated using pre-2026 data on crop patterns, livestock water use, and land productivity from Kenya and the Yellow River Basin studies, running scenarios to test policy and drought shocks.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, mechanistic framework to simulate the spatiotemporal propagation of water-use decisions, directly linking micro-level behaviors to macro-level resource outcomes. It will advance the field of water footprint analysis by moving from static accounting to dynamic, agent-driven scenario modeling, offering policymakers insights into leverage points and resilience in agricultural water systems.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of epidemiological network models to farmer decision cascades in water use is a novel interdisciplinary synthesis. It integrates the physical metrics of water productivity with the social dynamics of behavioral contagion, a combination not yet explored in Hoekstra's pre-2026 work on footprints and productivity.

proposals.title: Quantifying uncertainty in green and blue water partitioning for evapotranspiration: A high-frequency sensor and Bayesian mixing model approach
proposals.motivation: Accurate partitioning of evapotranspiration (ET) into green (rainwater) and blue (surface/groundwater) sources is critical for sustainable water resource management, especially in agricultural and livestock systems under increasing water scarcity. Current methods, including stable isotope mixing models, often treat source signatures as fixed values, ignoring their spatiotemporal variability and leading to unquantified uncertainty in water productivity assessments and future scenario projections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the isotopic and chemical signatures of all potential ET source waters as probability distributions within a hierarchical Bayesian framework will robustly quantify the uncertainty in green versus blue water partitioning and reveal the hydroclimatic conditions under which source discrimination is most reliable.
proposals.method: We will deploy a dense network of in-situ sensors in a representative agricultural catchment to collect high-frequency data on the isotopic (δ²H, δ¹⁸O) and chemical composition of precipitation, soil water at multiple depths, groundwater, and atmospheric vapor. These data will serve as dynamic end-members for a hierarchical Bayesian mixing model, implemented using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods (e.g., via the 'MixSIAR' package in R). The model will probabilistically partition ET sources, treating end-member values as distributions that vary in space and time, thereby directly propagating measurement and natural variability into the final uncertainty estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, uncertainty-quantified metric for green and blue water use in agro-ecosystems, directly enhancing the accuracy of water footprint and land productivity assessments for crops and livestock. It will deliver a transferable methodological framework that identifies when and where source partitioning is inherently ambiguous, thereby improving the reliability of water use scenarios and policy recommendations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of high-frequency, multi-source sensor data with a hierarchical Bayesian model that explicitly quantifies uncertainty, moving beyond deterministic single-value mixing models. This directly addresses a key methodological gap in water footprint and productivity studies led by Hoekstra's group, which have historically relied on more aggregated data and assumptions.

proposals.title: Field-scale validation and downscaling of grid-based water footprints: An IoT-sensor approach for precision in crop and livestock productivity assessment
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint (WF) and land productivity assessments, as applied in national and basin-scale studies, rely on aggregated, grid-based models (e.g., WaterGAP, CROPWAT) and statistical data. These models contain significant uncertainty at the field level, limiting their utility for informing precise on-farm water management and productivity improvements. Validating and refining these estimates with direct, high-resolution data is a critical step towards actionable sustainability metrics for farmers and policymakers.
proposals.hypothesis: That IoT sensor networks measuring soil moisture, microclimate, and crop growth at field scale will reveal systematic biases in grid-based WF estimates, enabling the development of robust downscaling algorithms that significantly improve the spatial accuracy of water productivity assessments for specific crops and livestock feed.
proposals.method: We will deploy a network of IoT sensor nodes (e.g., using LoRaWAN for connectivity) in selected crop and pasture fields in Kenya, measuring soil moisture (capacitive sensors), local precipitation, temperature, and NDVI (via simple spectral sensors). These high-frequency, geotagged data streams will be integrated into a cloud-based platform. We will use a data assimilation framework, specifically a modified Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), to fuse the IoT sensor data with the outputs of a field-level soil-water balance model, forcing it to converge on observed conditions. This calibrated model will then be used to generate true field-scale green and blue WFs, which will be statistically compared to downscaled outputs from the global grid-based WaterFootprint Assessment framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically validated protocol for downscaling continental water footprint models to the farm level, directly addressing the gap between macro-scale resource use indicators and local management. It will yield concrete correction factors and uncertainty ranges for WF estimates in key Kenyan agro-ecosystems, enhancing the credibility of virtual water flow and land productivity analyses for policy.
proposals.novelty: While IoT is used in precision agriculture, its systematic application to empirically ground-truth and correct the foundational datasets of water footprint science—a core tool in Hoekstra's research—is novel. The integration of real-time sensor data with a data assimilation framework specifically for WF downscaling moves beyond simple comparison to enable dynamic model correction.

proposals.title: Dynamic Water Footprints: A Decadal, High-Resolution Analysis of Crop and Livestock Production in the Yellow River Basin (1995-2025)
proposals.motivation: Water footprint assessments have predominantly relied on static, long-term averages (e.g., 1996-2005), masking critical temporal dynamics in water productivity and scarcity. In rapidly developing and climate-sensitive regions like the Yellow River Basin, understanding these year-to-year and decadal shifts is essential for robust scenario planning and sustainable resource allocation between agricultural sectors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that water footprints for key crops and livestock in the Yellow River Basin have exhibited significant non-linear trends and increased interannual variability over the last three decades, driven more by changes in agricultural productivity and management than by climate variability alone.
proposals.method: We will apply the Water Footprint Assessment framework at an annual time-step from 1995-2025. For crops, we will calculate annual blue and green water footprints using high-resolution climate data (CHIRPS, ERA5) and dynamic yield statistics, implementing the FAO-56 Penman-Monteith algorithm within a spatially explicit model. For livestock, we will trace annual footprints via dynamic feed baskets, linking crop footprint results to livestock production data. Statistical trend analysis (Mann-Kendall, Theil-Sen estimator) and decomposition analysis will isolate drivers of change.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first detailed, temporally explicit account of water footprint evolution for a major basin, directly extending Hoekstra's foundational static studies. It will generate novel insights into the volatility and trends of agricultural water use, offering a critical evidence base for evaluating the water implications of past policies and for designing resilient future scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the paradigm from static to dynamic water footprint accounting at high temporal resolution, and in explicitly analyzing the temporal decoupling between the physical volume and economic value of water use in agricultural trade, a dimension highlighted in prior work but not yet studied over time.

proposals.title: Trade-offs between agricultural water-land productivity and conservation: A scenario-based analysis for sustainable livelihood transitions in East Africa
proposals.motivation: Conservation policies in water-scarce regions often target agricultural land, potentially threatening the livelihoods of farmers and pastoralists. In the pre-2026 context of intensifying climate pressures and food security concerns, it is critical to quantitatively assess whether short-term economic losses from such policies can be offset by long-term gains in resource sustainability and productivity. This directly addresses the tension between Sustainable Development Goals for life on land (SDG 15) and zero hunger (SDG 2).
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic, spatially-targeted conservation interventions, which account for livestock and crop water-land productivity differentials, can lead to scenarios where initial livelihood impacts are minimized or become net-positive within a 20-year timeframe through enhanced ecosystem services and resource efficiency.
proposals.method: We will develop a spatially-explicit scenario analysis framework for a case study region in East Africa (e.g., the Tana River Basin). Using pre-2026 data, we will calculate water and land productivity metrics (m³/kg, m²/kg) for key crop and livestock systems. Scenarios will be constructed using GIS-based land-use change algorithms, simulating conservation set-asides under different rules (e.g., protecting low-productivity/high-erosion land first). A trade-off analysis will then model short-term livelihood impacts (value of production foregone) versus long-term gains from simulated improvements in water regulation and soil retention, using discounted economic valuation.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a quantitative, scenario-based tool for policymakers to evaluate the livelihood consequences of conservation. It will advance the field of water footprint assessment by integrating dynamic land-use scenarios and economic trade-off analysis, moving beyond static footprint accounting. The results will offer evidence-based pathways for sustainable resource use that explicitly balance ecological and human well-being objectives.
proposals.novelty: While Hoekstra's prior work has quantified water and land productivity, this proposal innovatively integrates these metrics into forward-looking conservation trade-off scenarios. It explicitly models the temporal dimension of livelihood impact versus ecological gain, a non-obvious synthesis of water footprint analysis, livelihood economics, and conservation planning.

proposals.title: Quantifying the annual economic losses from global forest and wetland degradation using an extended 'Sunken Billions' framework
proposals.motivation: The 'Sunken Billions' concept, developed by the World Bank to value losses from inefficient fisheries, provides a powerful lens to assess the economic cost of mismanaging global commons. Pre-2026, while water footprint and land productivity studies (like Hoekstra's work) quantify resource use, a systematic valuation of the annual economic losses from the degradation of other critical ecosystems like forests and wetlands is lacking. This gap hinders the formulation of compelling economic arguments for policy intervention to preserve these vital, non-market systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The annual economic losses from the unsustainable exploitation and degradation of global forests and wetlands are substantial, likely amounting to hundreds of billions of US dollars, and can be robustly quantified by adapting the 'Sunken Billions' methodology to account for their unique ecosystem service flows.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core 'Sunken Billions' algorithm, which compares current economic returns from a resource to an optimal, sustainable baseline. For forests and wetlands, we will define optimal baselines using pre-2026 scenarios of sustainable management (e.g., reduced deforestation rates, maintained hydrological function). The method will integrate spatially explicit data on ecosystem extent and degradation (e.g., from FAO and remote sensing) with economic valuation of key services (carbon sequestration, water regulation, biodiversity) from existing meta-analyses. Losses will be calculated as the difference between the net present value of ecosystem service flows under current degradation trajectories versus the optimal sustainable scenario, aggregated to a global annual figure.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first global, annualized estimate of economic losses from forest and wetland degradation, framing ecosystem decline as a critical macroeconomic inefficiency. It will extend the analytical framework of water and land productivity studies into the domain of explicit economic valuation, providing a unified metric (USD/year) to compare the cost of inaction across different ecosystems. The results will offer a tangible economic rationale for scaling up investments in natural capital restoration and sustainable management.
proposals.novelty: While ecosystem service valuations exist, applying the dynamic, counterfactual-based 'Sunken Billions' framework—originally for fisheries—to global forests and wetlands to calculate annual *losses* (not just static stocks) is novel. It moves beyond footprint accounting to quantify the ongoing economic waste of degradation, directly linking Hoekstra's resource productivity themes to macro-scale financial loss.

proposals.title: Assessing the full supply chain water footprint of bioenergy pathways in Kenya using Environmentally Extended Input-Output Analysis
proposals.motivation: Bioenergy is promoted for energy security and climate mitigation, but its water sustainability is contested. Existing water footprint assessments for crops like sugarcane or feedstocks for biogas often focus on direct irrigation, omitting significant indirect water use embedded in agricultural inputs (fertilizers, pesticides, machinery) and processing. A comprehensive, economy-wide assessment is needed to inform sustainable bioenergy policy and avoid shifting water scarcity burdens within supply chains.
proposals.hypothesis: The indirect water footprint of bioenergy production in Kenya, captured via upstream supply chains, constitutes a significant and previously under-quantified portion of its total water appropriation, varying substantially between different feedstock types and production systems.
proposals.method: We will apply Environmentally Extended Input-Output Analysis (EEIOA) using a pre-2026 Kenyan input-output table, extended with sectoral water withdrawal and consumption data from national statistics and literature. The analysis will quantify the total (direct and indirect) water footprint per monetary unit of output for key bioenergy-relevant sectors (e.g., sugarcane, maize, livestock for biogas). Specific bioenergy pathways will be modeled by linking these sectoral footprints to physical production data from recent livestock and crop productivity studies, following the structural path analysis method to trace key contributing supply chains.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first EEIOA-based, national-scale assessment of indirect water use for bioenergy in Kenya, moving beyond farm-gate analysis. It will yield a transparent mapping of water use hotspots in the bioenergy supply chain, offering policymakers a systemic tool to compare the water productivity of different energy crops and to evaluate trade-offs with food production and other land uses.
proposals.novelty: While EEIOA has been used for water footprints at national and sectoral levels, its explicit application to deconstruct and compare the full supply chain water footprints of specific bioenergy pathways within an African agro-economic context, integrating insights from recent livestock and crop water productivity studies, is novel.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis for Ranking Bioenergy Feedstock Systems Based on Water, Energy, and Economic Productivity
proposals.motivation: The expansion of bioenergy is critical for climate mitigation but poses significant risks to water and land resources, especially in water-scarce regions. Existing assessments, like water footprinting, often evaluate single criteria (e.g., water use per unit energy) in isolation, failing to capture the complex trade-offs between resource efficiency, energy output, and economic viability that are essential for sustainable policy and investment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) framework will reveal a significantly different ranking of bioenergy options (e.g., sugarcane, maize, jatropha, crop residues) compared to single-criterion assessments, by explicitly quantifying and weighting the trade-offs between water productivity, net energy yield, and economic cost.
proposals.method: The study will first define a set of relevant bioenergy feedstock systems and scenarios for a case study region (e.g., a water-stressed basin). For each system, we will calculate key performance indicators: water footprint (m3/GJ), net energy yield (GJ/ha), and levelized cost of energy ($/GJ). These will serve as criteria in an MCDA model, specifically using the Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS) algorithm. Stakeholder surveys will inform the weighting of criteria (e.g., water scarcity may increase the weight for water productivity). Sensitivity analysis will test the robustness of rankings to different weighting schemes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a transparent, integrated assessment tool for policymakers and investors to compare bioenergy pathways. It will advance the water footprint community's methodology by moving from single-metric accounting to multi-criteria evaluation, directly linking Hoekstra's water productivity concepts to energy and economic decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While MCDA is established in resource management, its application to rank bioenergy systems specifically by synthesizing water footprint, energy balance, and cost data into a single, weighted framework is novel. It transfers and adapts a decision-support methodology into the core domain of quantitative water and land productivity analysis pioneered by Hoekstra.

proposals.title: Spatial assessment of water-energy productivity for bioenergy crops: A dual footprint analysis for integrated resource policy
proposals.motivation: Water and energy are fundamentally linked, yet resource management often treats them in isolation. With growing pressure on freshwater resources and the need for sustainable bioenergy to meet climate targets, understanding the bioenergy output (GJ) per unit of water consumed (m³) is critical for efficient allocation. This dual productivity metric, building on the water footprint concept, is essential for identifying regions and crop systems where water use for energy production is most efficient, informing policies that avoid trade-offs between water security and renewable energy goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the water-energy productivity (GJ/m³) of bioenergy systems varies significantly by crop type, climatic region, and agricultural management, and that mapping this productivity reveals opportunities for optimizing virtual water and embodied energy flows in the global bioeconomy.
proposals.method: The research will apply and extend the water footprint assessment framework to calculate the green and blue water consumption of major bioenergy crops (e.g., maize, sugarcane, oil palm, short-rotation coppice) in key production regions. For each crop and region, the bioenergy output (GJ/ha) will be calculated based on established crop yield data and calorific conversion values. The core algorithm will compute the ratio of energy output to water input (WEP = GJ_yield / m³_WF). This will be integrated into a spatial scenario analysis, comparing current practices with improved water productivity scenarios, akin to methods used in recent livestock productivity studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit, crop-specific database of water-energy productivity, providing a tangible metric to assess the 'water cost of bioenergy.' It will contribute to the water footprint family of indicators by formulating its energy-centric dual, directly linking the fields of virtual water and embodied energy analysis. The results will offer a science-based tool for prioritizing bioenergy investments and policies that minimize water resource competition.
proposals.novelty: While water footprints of crops and energy footprints of production are well-studied, explicitly inverting the ratio to measure energy output per unit of water consumed represents a novel, integrative perspective on resource use efficiency. This dual problem formulation shifts the focus from minimizing water use per unit of biomass to maximizing useful energy output from a given volume of water, a non-obvious but critical optimization for the water-energy-food nexus.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Hierarchical Model for Spatiotemporally Explicit Crop Water Use Efficiency: Integrating Process-Guided Causality and Remote Sensing for Robust Water Footprint Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and productivity assessments, including our own work on livestock and crops in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin, often rely on static or regionally averaged water use efficiency (WUE) coefficients. This obscures critical spatiotemporal variability driven by environmental stressors (e.g., VPD, soil moisture), leading to high uncertainty in water use and virtual water flow estimates under changing climatic and management conditions. A causal, dynamic WUE framework is essential for targeting water productivity improvements and sustainable allocation in water-scarce regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian hierarchical model, which explicitly encodes causal physiological and hydrological drivers of WUE (e.g., the stomatal response to vapor pressure deficit and soil moisture) as priors, will yield more accurate and uncertaintly-quantified estimates of dynamic crop WUE from satellite data than current correlative or fixed-coefficient approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a process-guided Bayesian hierarchical model. The core will be a hierarchical representation of the underlying WUE process, linking satellite-derived GPP (e.g., from MODIS or Sentinel-2) and ET (e.g., from SSEBop or PML models) through a likelihood informed by the Penman-Monteith and light-use efficiency frameworks. Key drivers (VPD, soil moisture from SMAP, phenology) will be incorporated as covariates with structured priors derived from plant ecophysiology. The model will be implemented in Stan, enabling full uncertainty propagation from input errors through to posterior distributions of pixel-level WUE and aggregated water footprints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable method for estimating spatially and temporally variable crop WUE with quantified uncertainty, directly improving the robustness of water footprint accounting. It will advance the field of sustainable resource use by moving from descriptive footprinting to a diagnostic, causal framework that identifies the key levers (e.g., soil moisture deficits, atmospheric demand) influencing water productivity in different agro-ecological zones.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of causal, process-based ecophysiological constraints with a flexible Bayesian hierarchical model for remote sensing data, moving beyond statistical correlation to a mechanistic, uncertainty-aware estimation of WUE. This represents a significant departure from the constant WUE or purely empirical regression models dominant in pre-2026 water footprint literature.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Resilience Framework for Global Virtual Water Trade: An Analogy to Smart Power Grids
proposals.motivation: Global virtual water trade networks are crucial for mitigating regional water scarcity, but existing models treat them as static or slowly adjusting. In a pre-2026 context of increasing hydrological variability and extreme events, understanding the dynamic resilience of these networks to sudden shocks is critical. This research addresses the risk of cascading water scarcity failures triggered by localized droughts or trade disruptions, analogous to power grid blackouts.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling virtual water trade as a dynamic, self-adjusting network—akin to a smart power grid—will reveal a higher intrinsic resilience to shocks than static models suggest, but will also identify critical, non-redundant trade links whose failure could propagate scarcity through the global system.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model (ABM) coupled with a network optimization layer. The ABM represents major trading nations as agents responding to local water scarcity (modeled as reduced 'generation capacity') and global price signals. The optimization framework, adapting the Direct Current Optimal Power Flow (DCOPF) algorithm, will dynamically re-route virtual water flows to minimize 'scarcity cost' subject to network constraints (e.g., trade agreements, infrastructure limits). Scenarios will test the system's response to simulated hydrological shocks in key export regions for water-intensive commodities like crops and livestock.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dynamic assessment of global water trade resilience, moving beyond footprint accounting and static trade analysis. It will produce a policy-testing tool to identify leverage points for enhancing network robustness and preventing scarcity cascades, contributing directly to the discourse on sustainable global resource use under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, operational analogy to smart power grid engineering—applying concepts like dynamic rerouting, capacity constraints, and cascading failure—to virtual water trade modeling is a significant conceptual and methodological leap beyond existing economic or hydrological network studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Harmonized Multi-Decadal Reconstruction of National Water Footprints: Attributing Trends to Technological, Climatic, and Economic Drivers
proposals.motivation: Long-term analyses of water footprints (WFs) are essential for understanding historical pressures on freshwater resources and informing sustainable policies. However, existing time series are often inconsistent due to evolving methodologies, creating artifacts that obscure true trends. A harmonized reconstruction is needed to separate methodological noise from real drivers like efficiency gains, climate change, and shifting trade patterns, as highlighted in recent work on livestock and crop productivity in Kenya and the Yellow River Basin.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a single, consistent WF assessment framework to historical data will reveal that long-term trends in national water footprints are primarily driven by technological improvements in water productivity and changes in final consumption, with climate variability and international trade playing significant but secondary roles.
proposals.method: We will apply the Water Footprint Assessment framework consistently to historical (e.g., 1990-2020) data on crop/livestock production, climate, and trade for selected nations. Missing data will be addressed using validated multiple imputation techniques. A structural decomposition analysis (SDA) will be employed to attribute changes in the total WF to drivers: technological efficiency (water use per unit output), production structure, and final consumption. Counterfactual scenarios will isolate the impact of climatic shifts (using historical climate data) and trade pattern evolution.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce the first consistent, multi-decadal WF time series with quantified uncertainty, enabling robust trend analysis. It will provide a causal, quantitative attribution of historical WF changes to specific drivers, moving beyond descriptive accounting. This directly supports more targeted water policy and resource planning by identifying the key levers that have historically altered water demand.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the harmonized reconstruction that corrects for methodological inconsistencies across time, combined with the explicit driver attribution using decomposition analysis and counterfactual scenarios. This moves WF research from static accounting or simple trend description to a causal, historical driver analysis.

proposals.title: Structural Resilience and Keystone Nations in the Global Virtual Water Trade Network: An Ecological Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Global virtual water trade networks are critical for food and water security but are vulnerable to shocks from climate extremes, geopolitical conflicts, and trade disruptions. Pre-2026 research has quantified flows and footprints but lacks a systematic framework to assess the network's structural stability and identify systemically important nodes. Understanding this resilience is essential for developing robust resource management and trade policies.
proposals.hypothesis: The global virtual water trade network possesses structural properties analogous to ecological food webs, and applying stability analysis from theoretical ecology will reveal non-obvious, critical 'keystone' nations whose water resource stress or trade policy changes could disproportionately destabilize global water-for-food security.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed, weighted network for agricultural and livestock virtual water trade using FAO COMTRADE and Water Footprint Network data (circa 2010-2020). Adapting ecological network analysis, we will calculate structural metrics including connectance, modularity, and node centrality (e.g., weighted degree, betweenness). We will simulate cascading failure scenarios by sequentially removing or stressing high-centrality nodes (nations) and key trade links, modeling the propagation of water scarcity 'shocks' through the network to quantify systemic vulnerability.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first application of ecological stability theory to virtual water trade, offering a novel diagnostic framework to assess global water resource resilience. It will produce a prioritized list of critical nations and trade dependencies, informing risk assessments and policy debates on water security, trade diversification, and strategic reserves.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to trade, explicitly adapting food web stability concepts and cascading failure simulations to virtual water networks is a novel interdisciplinary synthesis. This moves beyond descriptive footprint accounting to a dynamic, systemic vulnerability analysis.

proposals.title: Minimizing Global Water Use Through Trade: An Inverse Water Footprint Optimization Using Electrical Circuit Flow Analogy
proposals.motivation: Current water footprint and virtual water flow studies, such as those for the Yellow River Basin or Kenyan livestock, quantify water use under existing, often inefficient, production and trade patterns. To move from accounting to strategic resource management, we need to identify the globally optimal allocation of production that minimizes water use for a given consumption bundle. This inverse problem is critical for informing policies on sustainable trade and water security, especially under growing resource constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling the global trade network as an electrical circuit where resistance represents water productivity, the production and trade flows that satisfy final demand can be optimized to minimize total system-wide water use, revealing significant potential savings compared to current patterns.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-commodity network model with nodes as economic regions and edges as trade pathways. Edge 'resistance' will be defined as the inverse of water productivity (m³/unit) for specific goods (e.g., crops, livestock products), incorporating regional data. Fixed net inflows at demand nodes will represent the consumption bundle. Using convex optimization techniques from network theory, specifically a linear programming formulation of the minimum-cost flow problem, we will solve for the flow distribution that minimizes aggregate water use (sum of flow² * resistance). The model will be applied to key agricultural and livestock commodities.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel normative framework for assessing the water-saving potential of global trade reorganization. It will yield quantitative benchmarks for 'best-possible' water use, against which current footprints can be evaluated, offering concrete targets for international water policy and sustainable supply chain design.
proposals.novelty: While water footprint networks are analyzed descriptively, applying the electrical circuit analogy and its associated optimization formalism to solve the inverse footprint problem is a novel methodological advance. It shifts the focus from 'what is' to 'what could be' in global water resource efficiency.

ideas.title: Agent-based co-evolution of crop patterns and irrigation decisions under dynamic water constraints
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated agent-based modeling framework that inverts traditional water planning. Starting from spatio-temporal profiles of available green and blue water, the model simulates a landscape of heterogeneous farmer agents who make adaptive crop choice and irrigation decisions based on these constraints, their own resources, and observed outcomes. The key innovation is the closed feedback loop: initial crop patterns emerge from water availability, but subsequent agent behaviors (e.g., deficit irrigation, technology adoption, water trading) modify blue water demand and recharge, dynamically updating the water constraints for the next season. This allows for simulating the co-evolution of agricultural systems and water resources under climate variability, identifying policies that guide the system toward sustainable equilibria.

ideas.description: This research develops a robust modeling system to estimate dynamic crop water use efficiency (WUE) by moving beyond simple correlation and embracing a causal, process-informed structure. It integrates satellite-derived physiological signals with principles from plant ecophysiology and soil hydrology within a Bayesian hierarchical model, explicitly representing the drivers of WUE variability (e.g., vapor pressure deficit, soil moisture stress, phenology). The framework systematically quantifies and propagates uncertainty from inputs through to final water footprint estimates, enabling reliable application across scales, even where direct ground observations are limited, by leveraging mechanistic constraints and spatial-temporal dependencies.

ideas.title: Adapting Sub-Pixel Unmixing from Remote Sensing to Model Sub-Grid Heterogeneity in Agricultural Footprints
ideas.description: This idea challenges the homogeneity assumption within coarse (5-arc-minute) agricultural grid cells by adapting the solution of sub-pixel unmixing from remote sensing. In satellite analysis, spectral mixture analysis quantifies the fractional cover of different land components within a single pixel. Adapted here, we propose a 'grid cell unmixing' framework where each grid cell's internal variability in soil, slope, and farm size is represented by a set of discrete, functional 'endmembers'. By leveraging higher-resolution global datasets to define the fractional area of these endmembers within each coarse cell, water balance and environmental footprint estimates can be refined as the area-weighted aggregate of the distinct processes occurring in each sub-grid land class, significantly improving the accuracy of large-scale agricultural models.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-quantified, sensor-integrated Bayesian framework for partitioning green and blue water sources in evapotranspiration
ideas.description: This approach deploys a dense network of in-situ sensors to continuously monitor the isotopic and chemical composition of potential water sources (precipitation, soil water at multiple depths, groundwater, surface water) and atmospheric water vapor. These high-frequency data feed into a hierarchical Bayesian mixing model that probabilistically partitions evapotranspiration (ET) sources. The framework explicitly models the spatial and temporal variability of end-member signatures as probability distributions rather than fixed values, directly quantifying the uncertainty in the green versus blue water contributions to ET and identifying conditions where source discrimination is robust versus inherently ambiguous.

ideas.title: Harmonized Multi-Decadal Water Footprint Reconstruction with Driver Attribution
ideas.description: This research reconstructs consistent water footprints over multiple decades by applying a unified computational model to historical raw input data, creating a harmonized time series. It explicitly quantifies uncertainty and corrects for gaps using validated imputation techniques to ensure comparability. The analysis then employs decomposition and counterfactual scenarios to attribute trends to specific drivers like technological efficiency, climatic shifts, trade patterns, and consumption changes. This approach isolates true historical drivers from methodological artifacts, producing a robust, causal understanding of long-term water resource impacts.

ideas.title: Spatial modeling of water pollution assimilation using ecological carrying capacity frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'carrying capacity' framework from wildlife ecology to water pollution regulation. In wildlife management, fixed population targets were replaced with spatially explicit models accounting for habitat heterogeneity to determine sustainable population levels. Similarly, this project proposes replacing global ambient water quality standards with locally variable natural assimilation capacities for grey water. By developing dynamic spatial models that incorporate local hydrological flow rates, microbial communities, temperature, oxygen levels, and geochemical conditions, we can map the actual pollution processing capacity of each water body segment. This would enable regulatory agencies to issue pollution discharge permits tailored to the specific resilience of each receiving ecosystem, creating a more scientifically grounded and environmentally protective regulatory system that acknowledges natural spatial variability in pollution assimilation.

ideas.title: Model nitrogen leaching as a dynamic state variable using a reservoir network approach
ideas.description: This idea challenges the constant nitrogen leaching fraction assumption by drawing an analogy from hydrology, where the constant runoff coefficient was successfully replaced by dynamic watershed models. The analogous solution conceptualizes a watershed as a network of interconnected reservoirs with state-dependent fluxes. Adapted to nitrogen cycling, the research would develop a model where the soil is represented as a set of linked nitrogen reservoirs (e.g., mineral, immobilized, subsoil). Nitrogen leaching is no longer a fixed fraction of inputs but a dynamic flux calculated by transfer functions that depend on the real-time state of these reservoirs, which are in turn governed by soil properties, climate drivers (precipitation, temperature), and management practices (irrigation, fertilizer timing). This allows leaching to emerge dynamically from the simulated interactions within the soil-plant-climate system.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse footprint optimization problem via electrical circuit flow analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the electrical circuit power loss minimization problem to solve the inverse footprint optimization problem. In the analogous problem, engineers determine current flows in a resistive network to deliver required power with minimal total I²R heat loss. Here, the global trade network is recast as a circuit: nodes are economic regions, edges are trade pathways with a 'resistance' parameter representing the direct and indirect water use per unit of good produced and transported. The given consumption bundle defines fixed net inflows at demand nodes. Using linear or convex optimization techniques from network theory (e.g., minimum-cost flow algorithms), the research will identify the specific pattern of production and trade flows that satisfies the consumption bundle while minimizing aggregate global water use, effectively finding the most water-efficient allocation of production tasks worldwide.

ideas.title: Data-assimilative hybrid model for dynamic nitrogen leaching with uncertainty quantification
ideas.description: This research develops a robust, operational model for dynamic nitrogen leaching by fusing a parsimonious process core with a flexible data assimilation system. The framework is designed to ingest and harmonize disparate, real-world data streams—from remote sensing to farm records—to continuously update model states and reduce parametric uncertainty. It explicitly quantifies and outputs prediction confidence intervals for leaching fluxes, providing actionable insights despite inherent data gaps. This approach ensures the model remains practical, testable, and more reliable than either a fixed fraction or a complex, unconstrained dynamic model.

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemiological Network Models for Farmer Decision Cascades in Water Use
ideas.description: This idea adapts agent-based modeling solutions from epidemiology, where network models simulate disease spread via individual states and contact-based transmission rules. The analogous solution involves agents (individuals) in a social network changing states (e.g., SIR model) based on local interactions and adaptive behaviors. Adapted to farmer irrigation decisions, agents (farmers) are embedded in a dual network of physical water infrastructure and social information exchange. Their irrigation strategy 'state' changes in response to water availability signals, perceived economic risk, and, crucially, the observed behaviors of neighboring farmers, mimicking complex contagion. This model simulates how technological adoption, policy interventions, or drought shocks propagate, capturing non-linear tipping points and feedbacks in regional blue water use that simpler aggregate models miss.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Food Web Stability Analysis to the Virtual Water Trade Network
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology. The analogous problem is understanding the stability and resilience of complex ecological food webs, which are solved by modeling them as interaction networks and applying stability analysis metrics and simulations. This solution is adapted by conceptualizing the global virtual water trade network as an ecological network, where countries are nodes and trade flows are directed links. The adapted research will apply ecological stability analysis techniques—such as measuring modularity, connectance, and simulating the cascading effects of node or link failure—to quantify the structural resilience of the virtual water trade system and identify critical, vulnerable, or 'keystone' nations whose disruption could destabilize the global network.

ideas.title: Model Virtual Water Trade as a Resilient Power Grid
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that virtual water trade networks are static or slowly changing by treating them as dynamic, resilient systems analogous to modern smart power grids. Drawing from electrical power systems engineering, where grids dynamically reroute electricity in response to faults and demand signals, we adapt these principles to model global virtual water trade. The research would develop an agent-based or network optimization framework where trade flows are continuously re-calculated in response to hydrological scarcity shocks (modeled as loss of generation capacity) and economic price signals. This allows for simulating the network's capacity to prevent cascading water scarcity, identify critical, failure-prone trade links, and test policies that enhance the system's resilience, much like grid operators manage power flow to prevent blackouts.
